stem: Patient s/p MVA. What is the best description for the pattern of injury shown?
explanation: Answer A. Flexion-distraction injury.

stem: Patient s/p MVA. Which of the following is associated with the above injury?

options.text: Mechanically stable injury without any associated injuries.
Most patients have significant neurologic injuries.
Extreme hyperextension resulting in dorsal soft tissue injury.

explanation:
Answer C. Commonly associated with visceral injuries. Images courtesy of Dr. Nafi Aygun Imaging Findings: Sagittal CT image shows a transverse fracture through the vertebral body and pedicle (arrow A). Sagittal T2-weighed MRI shows disruption of the ligamentum flavum (arrow B), adjacent interspinous edema, and widening. Note there is also a dorsal epidural hematoma (dotted arrow). Per AO classification, this would be classified as B2 injury. Discussion Chance Fracture, Spinal Injury: Chance fractures are unstable, three-column injuries resulting from flexion-distraction forces such as in seat belt injury. About 30% of polytrauma patients have a spinal injury. Spinal cord injury occurs in 15% to 30% of traumatic spinal fractures. These fractures are frequently located at the thoracolumbar junction for biomechanical reasons. Reasons for the Susceptibility of the Thoracolumbar Transition to Trauma • Transition from rigid thoracic kyphosis to more mobile lumbar lordosis. • Lower thoracic ribs (T11 and T12) provide less stability as they do not connect to the sternum. • Change in the orientation of thoracic and lumbar facet joints. Primary Goals of Treatment • Prevention of neurologic injury • Restoration of spinal stability Secondary Goals of Treatment • Correction of deformities • Minimizing the loss of function • Facilitating rapid rehabilitation Pathomechanisms of Injury • Several forces act together to produce structural damage to the spine. Most frequently, 1 or 2 forces define the injury pattern. The most common forces areAxial compression • Flexion-distraction • Hyperextension • Rotation • Shear Axial Compression Axial loading results in anterior flexion forces in the kyphotic thoracic spine and mainly compressive forces in the straight thoracolumbar region. • Endplate fracture • Vertebral body compression/wedge compression • Burst fracture With more severe injuries, posterior ligamentous and osseous elements may fail, then would be classified as flexion-distraction injury. Flexion/Distraction The axis of flexion is more anterior, and the entire vertebral column is subject to tensile forces. Forces cause tension in the posterior elements and compression of vertebral bodies. If anterior wedging exceeds 40% to 50%, rupture of the posterior ligaments and facet joint capsules is likely. This process can produce • Pure osseous lesion (Chance fracture) • Mixed osteoligamentous lesion • Pure soft tissue (ligamentous or disk) lesion They carry high association with visceral organ injury. Hyperextension Occurs when the upper part of the trunk is stressed posteriorly. The injury pattern is the reverse of that seen with flexion. Tension is applied anteriorly to the anterior longitudinal ligament and anterior portion of the annulus fibrosis and compressive forces to the posterior elements. Anterior discoligamentous disruption and posterior compression fractures involving facets, lamina, or spinous processes. Rotational Injuries Combination of compressive forces and flexion/distraction mechanisms. Typically, unstable injuries. Shear Severe ligamentous disruption and may result in displacement. Often associated with spinal cord injury. Classification Denis three-column classification Anterior column (consists of anterior longitudinal ligament, anterior annulus fibrosus, and anterior half of the vertebral bodies). Middle column (consists of posterior longitudinal ligament, dorsal annulus fibrosus, and dorsal half of the vertebral body). Posterior column (consists of the bony neural arch, posterior spinous ligaments, ligamentum flavum, and facet joints). The middle column is the key structure for stability. This classification does not allow for a detailed fracture classification. AO classification Currently, the most used classification system.Three categories based on morphology. Neurological status modifier • N0: Neurologically intact • N1: Transient deficit, resolved • N2: Radiculopathy • N3: Incomplete spinal cord injury or cauda equina I syndrome • N4: Complete spinal cord injury • NX: Cannot be examined Patient-specific modifiers • M1: indeterminate tension band injury • M2: Comorbidities that may affect patient care, for example, ankylosing spondylitis Points are given based on injury type, neurologic status and patient-specific modifiers, with surgical considerations typically suggested for 4 or more overall points. Imaging Workup Any static imaging study is a snapshot in time. Most imaging is done in the prone position that lacks physiologic load and may underestimate the severity/instability of the injury. Radiographs are often of poor quality. CT is the investigation of choice for bony injury. MRI is helpful in evaluating discoligamentous lesions and cord injury.

References: Bernstein MP, Mirvis SE, Shanmuganathan K. Chance-type fractures of the thoracolumbar spine: imaging analysis in 53 patients. AJR Am J Roentgenol. 2006;187(4):859-868. Dane B, Bernstein MP. Imaging of spine trauma. Semin Roentgenol. 2016;51(3):180-202. Boos N, Aebi M, eds. Spinal Disorders: Fundamentals of Diagnosis and Treatment. Springer; 2008. https://www.aofoundation.org/spine/clinical-library-and-tools/aospine-classification-systems

stem: A 50-year-old woman with acute midthoracic back pain presented to the ER. An MRI was performed with intravenous contrast; key images are shown below. Further clinical workup did not reveal any signs of symptoms to suggest infection. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Answer A. Schmorl node. This study shows endplate defect involving the central aspect with corresponding enhancement. Note relatively rounded nodular morphology invaginating along the endplates on the pre- and postcontrast T1w sequences, typical for Schmorl node formation. Edema and enhancement can be seen in acute/subacute stages. Occasionally, appearance can be indistinguishable on imaging from diskitis/osteomyelitis especially early in the disease course, and therefore, interpretation in the clinical context is very important.

stem: A 50-year-old woman with acute midthoracic back pain presented to the ER. An MRI was performed with intravenous contrast; key images are shown below. Which of the following disorders is associated with the above condition in younger individuals?
explanation:
Answer C. Scheuermann disease. A condition of abnormal kyphosis typically seen in the thoracic spine in the prepubertal age group, it has been associated with multiple endplate Schmorl node formation. The other conditions also have skeletal manifestations, but none of them have a specific predisposition toward Schmorl node formation. Imaging Findings: Sagittal pre- (A) and postcontrast T1w image (B) shows herniation of disk material through the inferior endplate (arrow A) with corresponding enhancement (arrow B). This is compatible with acute/subacute Schmorl node formation. Schmorl Node Discussion: Schmorl nodes are disk herniations into the endplates, either into the weakened cartilaginous endplates or within the subchondral bone. The weakened zones provide the least resistance to expansion of the nucleus pulposus, especially in younger individuals with intact annulus fibrosis. The herniated material typically comprises the nucleus pulposus with an associated degenerative or inflammatory response. In the chronic phases, there is characteristic sclerosis along the abutting vertebral spongiosa. In acute/subacute stages, edema and enhancement can be present. If these are more pronounced, it could become difficult to differentiate them from diskitis/osteomyelitis. Most of these lesions are asymptomatic; however, occasionally patients can present with pain. Schmorl's nodes with adjacent marrow edema tend to have a higher correlation with symptoms. These are conservatively managed with symptomatic treatment. Key Point: Schmorl node refers to intrabody disk herniation, generally an incidental finding.

References: Grivé E, Rovira A, Capellades J, et al. Radiologic findings in two cases of acute Schmörl's nodes. AJNR Am J Neuroradiol. 1999;20(9):1717-1721. Wagner AL, Murtagh FR, Arrington JA, et al. Relationship of Schmorl's nodes to vertebral body endplate fractures and acute endplate disk extrusions. AJNR Am J Neuroradiol. 2000;21(2):276-281.

stem: A 34-year-old male presents with pain in his lower neck after a weight-lifting accident in the gym. There was focal tenderness on palpation in the lower cervical spine on examination. The patient was neurologically intact. A radiograph of the cervical spine was performed, provided below. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Possible facet fracture; CT is recommended.

explanation:
Answer C. Clay shoveler's fracture. There is a fracture of C7 spinous process. The facets are intact. Imaging Findings: Lateral cervical radiograph: Oblique fracture of C7 spinous process. AP radiograph demonstrates double spinous process of C7 due to inferior displacement of fracture fragment. Clay Shoveler's Fracture Discussion: Clay shoveler's fracture. This is an avulsion fracture of the spinous process, most commonly C7. It can result from both hyperflexion and hyperextension injury as well as direct trauma to the spinous processes. It is mostly seen on x-ray/CT as an oblique fracture of the spinous process midway between the tip and lamina with posterior-inferior displacement of distal fracture fragment. On frontal radiograph, displaced fracture fragment appears as double spinous process of the involved vertebra. Management is mostly conservative as this injury in isolation is neurologically stable.

References: Posthuma de Boer J, van Wulfften Palthe AF, Stadhouder A, et al. The clay shoveler's fracture: a case report and review of the literature. J Emerg Med. 2016;51(3):292-297.

stem: Key images from the MRI performed for evaluation of back pain in the setting of a fall are provided below. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Answer C. Compression fracture and epidural hematoma.

stem: Key images from the MRI performed for evaluation of back pain in the setting of a fall are provided below. Which of the following is correct regarding the above entity?
explanation:
Answer A. Urgent neurosurgery consultation. Imaging Findings: Sagittal T1, sagittal T2, and sagittal T1w fat-saturated postcontrast images demonstrate abnormal T1 and T2 hyperintense collection in the posterior epidural space (solid arrow A and C). Note acute disk herniation (dotted arrow, B) with hemorrhage/edema within the herniated disk. Acute compression fracture of L2, postvertebroplasty changes in L3, and a chronic compression fracture of T12 are also seen. Spinal Epidural Hematoma Discussion: Spinal epidural hematoma. Hemorrhage in the spinal epidural space may be traumatic or nontraumatic in etiology. MRI is the most sensitive imaging modality for detection. MR signal depends on the age of the hemorrhage. Acute blood is isointense on T1 and hyperintense on T2w images, whereas in the subacute stage, methemoglobin results in T1 high signal as seen in the case above. Fat-saturated sequences differentiate it from epidural lipomatosis, and administration of gadolinium helps differentiate from enhancing epidural abnormalities including epidural phlegmon and neoplastic etiologies such as lymphoma and metastases. Spinal epidural hematomas can result in devastating neurologic outcomes if unrecognized. Symptomatic patients often require urgent decompression to preserve neurologic function.

References: Al-Mutair A, Bednar DA. Spinal epidural hematoma. J Am Acad Orthop Surg. 2010;18(8):494-502. Braun P Kazmi K, Nogués-Meléndez P, Mas-Estellés F, Aparici-Robles F. MRI findings in spinal subdural and epidural hematomas. Eur J Radiol. 2007;64(1):119-125.

stem: Regarding the following images, select the best option.
explanation:
Answer A. Flexion teardrop fracture. Imaging Findings: Multiple sagittal images of the cervical spine demonstrate a triangular bone fragment (solid arrow A) at the anteroinferior C4 vertebral body, along with widening of interspinous distance, widened facet joint (solid arrow B), as well as widened C4-C5 disk space suggestive of a three-column injury. Per AO spine classification this would be C4-C5 C (C referring to translation), C4 A1 (A referring to compression), F4 (F4 referring to facet subluxation). There is severe prevertebral soft tissue swelling (C). Also, note the epidural hematoma spanning the entire cervical canal best seen in image C. Flexion Teardrop Fracture Discussion: Flexion teardrop fracture results from severe hyperflexion and axial compression injury of the cervical spine such as diving head-first into a shallow pool. "Teardrop" refers to the triangular shape of the bone fragment at the anteroinferior (more common) or posteroinferior angle of the vertebral body typically C5/C6. Hyperflexion injury results in disruption of the posterior ligaments with progressive injury resulting in posterior longitudinal ligament and diskal disruption from posterior to anterior. A fracture of the anterior vertebral body completes the three-column injury. These injuries are often associated with retropulsion of the posterior vertebral body fragment resulting in a mass effect on the anterior spinal cord. Additional vertebral body and posterior element fractures are often seen. It is important to recognize subtle signs of posterior ligamentous disruption on CT imaging, especially in patients with a cervical collar. Look for widened interspinous distance, incongruity or widening of facet joints, and asymmetric widening of the disk in addition to osseous injuries. Lastly, attention should always be paid to the prevertebral and epidural soft tissues to detect any obvious hemorrhage such as in the case shown. AO Spine Classification for Subaxial Injury A, B, and C injuries are like thoracolumbar classification (see discussion for question 1 in this section). If the injury is limited to facets or if facet injury is a major component of the injury, then type F classification is used. Neurological status modifier • N0: Neurologically intact • N1: Transient deficit, resolved • N2: Radiculopathy • N3: Incomplete spinal cord injury or cauda equina I syndrome • N4: Complete spinal cord injury • NX: Cannot be examined • +: continued spinal cord compression Patient-specific modifiers • M1: Posterior capsuloligamentous complex injury without complete disruption • M2: Critical disk herniation • M3: Stiffening/metabolic bone disease (e.g., DISH, AS, OPLL, OLF) • M4: Vertebral artery abnormality

References: Bari D, Bernstein MP. Imaging of spine trauma. Semin Roentgenol. 2016;51(3):180-202. Kim KS, Chen HH, Russell EJ, et al. Flexion teardrop fracture of the cervical spine: radiographic characteristics. AJR Am J Roentgenol. 1989;152(2):319-326. https://www.aofoundation.org/spine/clinical-library-and-tools/aospine-classification-systems

stem: Patient presents with trauma. What is the hallmark of the above injury?
explanation: Answer A. Bilateral C2 pedicle fractures. These fractures are often described as "hangman's fractures."

stem: Patient presents with trauma. Which additional imaging investigation is warranted in this patient?

options.text: CT neck soft tissues with contrast

explanation:
Answer A. CT angiogram of the neck. Extension through bilateral transverse foramen is seen, indicating the need for CTA or MRA neck for assessment of potential vertebral artery injury. Imaging Findings: There is a linear fracture seen along bilateral C2 pedicles (A and B). Note that the fracture margin extends into the transverse foramen. Therefore, a CTA or MRA neck to evaluate for potential vertebral artery injury is necessary. Hangman's Fracture Discussion: Hangman's fracture or traumatic pedicle/pars interarticularis fractures of C2. It is typically seen with hyperextension and distraction such as with hanging; however, it may result from both hyperflexion and hyperextension injury. There are three types based on the extent of translation and angulation (Effendi classification): Type 1: Minimal translation (<2 mm, no angulation) Type 2: Anterior translation with anterior angulation of more than 11 degrees Type 2A: Severe angulation without anterior translation Type 3: Bilateral fracture dislocation Look for associated fractures of odontoid and C1. Although classically described as pars fractures, any part of the C2 ring including the posterior vertebral body, pedicles, and laminae may be involved. AO spine classification places upper cervical injury into three classes based on the location. • Occipital condyle and craniocervical junction • C1 ring and C1-C2 joint • C2 and C2-C3 joint Within these classes, there are three types based on injury pattern. • Type A: Bony injury only • Type B: Tension band/ligamentous injury (bony and/or ligamentous injury) • Type C: Translation injury Neurologic modifiers are like thoracolumbar and subaxial cervical spine classifications (refer to the discussion earlier in this chapter). Modifiers: • M1-injuries at high risk of nonunion with nonoperative treatment • M2-injuries with significant potential for instability • M3-patient-specific factors affecting treatment (like age, comorbidities, neuro status, smoking, other injuries, ankylosing spondylitis) • M4-vascular injury or an abnormality affecting treatment

References: Dane B, Bernstein MP. Imaging of spine trauma. Semin Roentgenol. 2016;51(3):180-202. David D, Letzing M, Sliker CW, et al. Multidetector CT of blunt cervical spine trauma in adults. Radiographics. 2014;34(7):1842-1865.

stem: A 27-year-old male with motor vehicle accident, key images are shown below. What is the most common mechanism of injury to cause the fracture shown?
explanation: Answer D. Axial loading.

stem: A 27-year-old male with motor vehicle accident, key images are shown below. Which of the following C1 fractures is considered most unstable?

options.text: Burst fracture with intact transverse ligament
Burst fracture with disruption of the transverse ligament

explanation:
Answer D. Burst fracture with disruption of transverse ligament. Imaging Findings: Images A and B show fractures through anterior and posterior arch of C1. Coronal image through the cervical spine demonstrates offset of the lateral masses of C1 and C2. Per AO spine classification, this would be C1 ring and C1-C2 joint injury, type B (given the subluxation of joints) and like a modifier of M2. Jefferson Fracture Discussion: Jefferson fracture. Burst fracture of C1 (Atlas) resulting from axial loading injury. C1 is a ring-shaped vertebra that comprises the anterior arch, posterior arch, and two lateral masses, which articulate superiorly with occipital condyles and inferiorly with lateral masses of C2. The classic Jefferson fracture is a four-part fracture with four fractures, two involving the junction of the anterior arch with the lateral masses and two fractures at the junction of the posterior arch and lateral masses. Isolated arch fractures or lateral mass fractures may also occur. Most patients remain neurologically intact. Clinical management often depends on the integrity of the transverse atlantal ligament, which is instrumental in preserving atlantoaxial stability. This can be assessed directly with MRI. On open-mouth view radiographs or coronal CT, a sum of lateral displacement of lateral masses of C1 over C2 exceeding 7 mm is considered a sign of transverse ligament injury (rule of Spence). Congenital fusion anomalies of the anterior or posterior arch may mimic fractures but can be differentiated by their well-corticated edges.

References: Dane B, Bernstein MP. Imaging of spine trauma. Semin Roentgenol. 2016;51(3):180-202. Dreizin D, Letzing M, Sliker CW, et al. Multidetector CT of blunt cervical spine trauma in adults. Radiographics. 2014;34(7):1842-1865.

stem: Which of the following is shown on the image below?
explanation:
Answer C. Type 2 odontoid fracture. There is a fracture at the base of the dens without extension of the fracture line into the body of C2, compatible with a type 2 odontoid fracture. Imaging Findings: CT image through the cervical spine demonstrates a nondisplaced fracture through the base of the odontoid process. Fracture morphology is in keeping with type II odontoid fracture. Per AO spine classification, this would be C2 and C2-C3 joint region injury, type A, M1. Odontoid Fracture Discussion: Odontoid fracture is the most common fracture of C2. They may result from hyperflexion or hyperextension injuries. It is common in the elderly even with low-energy trauma. Types of odontoid fracture (Anderson and D'Alonzo classification): Type 1: Avulsion fracture of the alar ligament involving the tip of the odontoid. Type 2: Fracture through the base of dens; usually unstable Type 2a: Type 2 fracture with comminution at the odontoid base. Type 3: Fracture through the base of dens with extension into the body of C2. Best prognosis for healing. Differential Diagnosis: It is important to differentiate odontoid fractures from common variants at this location. Os odontoideum: Well-corticated ossicle in association with a hypoplastic odontoid process, often accompanied by hypertrophy of the C1 anterior arch. Ossiculum terminale: Small well-corticated bone just superior to the odontoid tip with a normal-appearing dens.

References: Dreizin D, Letzing M, Sliker CW, et al. Multidetector CT of blunt cervical spine trauma in adults. Radiographics. 2014;34(7):1842-1865. Munera F, Rivas LA, Nunez DB Jr, et al. Imaging evaluation of adult spinal injuries: emphasis on multidetector CT in cervical spine trauma. Radiology. 2012;263(3):645-660.

stem: A 75-year-old postmenopausal women with severe lower back pain had an MRI evaluation. Key images are shown below. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Answer D. Sacral insufficiency fracture. This is the most likely diagnosis in view of the morphology, location, and patient demographics. Imaging Findings: Coronal T1w image demonstrates edema-like marrow signal abnormality with T1 hypointense band (A) and STIR (B) hyperintensity in the sacral ala. The fracture line is seen as a bright line parallel to the sacroiliac joint (B). Sacral Insufficiency Fracture Discussion: Sacral insufficiency fracture (SIF). An insufficiency fracture results from normal stress on a weakened/osteopenic bone most commonly secondary to osteoporosis or metabolic bone disease. Sacral insufficiency fracture is a relatively underdiagnosed cause of severe back pain, especially in the elderly. MRI is almost 100% sensitive in detecting these fractures. Although classically described as an H-shaped fracture with vertical fractures of bilateral sacral ala and horizontal fracture through the body (Honda sign on bone scintigraphy), unilateral fractures are common. The most conspicuous imaging finding is the detection of marrow edema. The fracture lines itself may be seen as dark bands on both T1- and T2-weighted images. If fluid filled, fracture lines can appear T2 bright. Although not as sensitive, CT imaging reveals these fractures as sclerotic bands lateral to the neural foramina and parallel to the sacroiliac joints. Interruption of the anterior sacral cortex may be seen in some cases. It is imperative to scrutinize the sacral ala on MR images of the lumbar spine as well as on CT of abdomen images to recognize unsuspected cases of this entity. Treatment is mostly conservative with bed rest and pain control. The role of sacroplasty (akin to vertebroplasty) remains under evaluation.

References: Diel J, Ortiz O, Losada RA, et al. The sacrum: pathologic spectrum, multimodality imaging, and subspecialty approach. Radiographics. 2001;21:83-104. Lyders EM, Whitlow CT, Baker MD, et al. Imaging and treatment of sacral insufficiency fractures. AJNR Am J Neuroradiol. 2010;31(2):201-210.

stem: What is the most likely cause for cervical cord abnormality in this patient?
explanation:
Answer B. Traumatic cord contusion/edema. Although cord signal changes can be seen with any of the entities given as choices in this question, the presence of an acute fracture and expansion of the cord is most consistent with cord contusion in this patient. A T2*GRE sequence can be performed to assess potential blood products and if present would make this a contusion. Imaging Findings: Sagittal T2w image reveals fractures of C6 and C7 vertebral bodies with anterolisthesis, retropulsion, and disk injury, resulting in spinal cord injury. Spinal Cord Contusion Discussion: Spinal cord contusion or spinal cord injury (SCI) is among the most devastating sequelae of blunt spinal trauma. Preexisting spinal canal narrowing, either congenital or secondary to spondylosis, increases the likelihood of cord trauma. MRI is the investigation for choice for spinal cord injury. Five basic patterns of cord injury have been described with MRI. These include the following: Cord swelling: Increased cord diameter without signal alteration. Cord edema: Swelling of the cord at the site of injury with increased T2 signal. Cord contusion: Hemorrhagic foci (T2 isointense/hypointense foci and susceptibility on gradient images) with surrounding edema. Cord hemorrhage: T2 hypointensity with a surrounding rim of high signal; associated susceptibility. Increased T1 signal is typically not seen in the acute phase but may be detected after 3 days once methemoglobin starts to form. Cord transection: Very rare with blunt trauma. The sensitivity for depicting intramedullary hemorrhage can be enhanced by obtaining T2* images. In terms of clinical outcome, cord swelling, and edema are associated with better neurologic recovery compared to cord contusion and hemorrhage. Although imaging findings may revert to normal in patients with cord edema, those with cord hemorrhage often progress to myelomalacia. Injury cascade with a sequence of ischemia, hypoperfusion, reperfusion, and ensuing oxidative damage can trigger a secondary injury. Progressive posttraumatic myelomalacic myelopathy is an uncommon sequel of spinal cord injury seen in <2% of cases. It manifests with myelomalacic changes/cyst formation noncontiguous to the site of the initial trauma. Subacute progressive ascending myelopathy (SPAM) is another rare sequel of SCI. Patients present with ascending neurologic deficit within 3 weeks of the initial injury. MRI demonstrates abnormal T2 signal and edema spanning at least 4 segments proximal to the site of injury.

References: Goldberg AL, Kershah SM. Advances in imaging of vertebral and spinal cord injury. J Spinal Cord Med. 2010;33(2):105-116. Mahmood NS, Kadavigere R, Ramesh AK, et al. Magnetic resonance imaging in acute cervical spinal cord injury: a correlative study on spinal cord changes and 1 month motor recovery. Spinal Cord. 2008;46:791- 797.

stem: A 72-year-old female with history of breast cancer presents with neck pain. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for the images shown above?
explanation: Answer D. Pathologic compression fracture. Note the soft tissue component in the epidural space, enhancing vertebral body, and lack of abutting disk enhancement. This appearance in the setting of breast cancer is compatible with a metastatic disease-related pathologic compression fracture.

stem: A 72-year-old female with history of breast cancer presents with neck pain. Which of the following features favors the diagnosis of a benign compression fracture?
explanation:
Answer D. Fluid-filled cleft on T2-weighted images. This finding has been associated with benign osteoporotic compression fractures. Imaging Findings: Sagittal T1-weighted precontrast image (left) demonstrates flattened C7 vertebral body with diffuse marrow replacement and convex posterior border. Postcontrast T1w image depicts diffuse marrow enhancement as well as enhancing ventral epidural soft tissue component. Findings are compatible with pathologic compression fracture. Pathological Fracture Discussion: Vertebra plana refers to the morphologic appearance of a flattened vertebral body with normal adjacent disk spaces. It can be secondary to benign etiologies such as osteoporosis, trauma, and infection, as well as malignant causes including metastatic disease, lymphoma, and myeloma. In the pediatric population, eosinophilic granuloma is the most common cause of vertebra plana. Differentiating benign from malignant causes is not always straightforward. Some of the imaging features that favor a benign etiology include the following: Presence of fracture lines resulting from trabecular compression seen as dark bands on T1-/T2-weighted images • A fluid-filled cleft on T2w images • Preserved normal marrow signal • Preserved posterior contour of the vertebral body • Absence of an associated soft tissue mass • Marrow edema tends to be inhomogeneous in the acute/subacute stages • Lack of bone destruction Pathologic compression fractures often demonstrate • More homogenous marrow replacement • Bone destruction • Convex bulging of the posterior vertebral body secondary to an intraosseous mass • Absence of fracture lines (as normal trabeculae are replaced by an infiltrating tumor) • Presence of enhancing prevertebral or epidural soft tissue mass • Marrow signal abnormality extending into posterior elements • Presence of other spinal masses Fractures associated with multiple myeloma can be indistinguishable from osteoporotic fractures. The presence of enhancement within the vertebral body may be seen in both benign and malignant compression fractures.

References: Baur A, Stäbler A, Arbogast S, et al. Acute osteoporotic and neoplastic vertebral compression fractures: fluid sign at MR imaging. Radiology. 2002;225(3):730-735. Jung HS, Jee WH, McCauley TR, et al. Discrimination of metastatic from acute osteoporotic compression spinal fractures with MR imaging. Radiographics. 2003;23(1):179-187.

stem: A 65-year-old male with low back pain and lower extremity radiculopathy undergoes an MRI for further evaluation. Key images from MRI are shown below. Based on the above images, which nerve root might explain the patient's symptoms?
explanation: Answer A. Left L3. The images show soft tissue density in the left L3-L4 extraforaminal zone. This is compatible with an extraforaminal disk protrusion encroaching on the exiting left L3 nerve root.

stem: A 65-year-old male with low back pain and lower extremity radiculopathy undergoes an MRI for further evaluation. Key images from MRI are shown below. What is the best term to describe the anatomic location of the abnormality?
explanation:
Answer C. Left L3 extraforaminal zone. The disk herniation involves the proximal extraforaminal zone abutting the exiting left L3 nerve root. Imaging Findings: There is a small disk protrusion involving the left extraforaminal zone (solid arrow A and B) that abuts the exiting L3 nerve root (dotted arrow L3). Disk Herniation Discussion: This case refers to the usage of the lumbar disk nomenclature version 2.0, employed to standardize the reporting of lumbar spine degenerative abnormalities. The term disk "herniation" is reserved for the displacement of disk material beyond the edges of vertebral apophysis in a focal or localized fashion (<25% of disk circumference). This is in contrast to a bulge that extends >25% of the disk circumference. The following types of herniations can be present: • Disk protrusion: A type of herniation where the distance between the edges of the herniated disk material is less than the distance between the edges of the base (at the site of connection with the disk of origin). • Disk extrusion: A type of herniation where the distance between the edges of the herniated disk material is more than the distance between the edges of the base in at least any one plane (note that the measurement for the base and herniated disk edges must be made on the same plane). If there is no continuity between the extruded disk material and the disk of origin, this latter subtype of extrusion is referred to as a "sequestered" disc. • Accurately designating the site of disk herniation and impact on the nerve roots at that level is essential. The anatomic sites for designating disc herniations are central, subarticular, foraminal and extraforaminal zones. Axial and sagittal planes on a T2-weighted MRI of the cervical spine is shown above. This shows the nerve roots exiting at the C2-C3 junction are C3 nerve roots. Note that the cervical spinal nerve roots exit superior to their vertebral levels until the C8 nerve roots that exit at C7-T1, superiorly to the first thoracic vertebrae. As follows, the rest of the spinal nerve roots in the thoracic and lumbar spine exit inferiorly to their corresponding named vertebrae. Key Point: Following lumbar disk nomenclature, version 2.0 is essential for consistent terminology and standardized assessment for lumbar spine degenerative disease reporting.

References: Fardon DF, Williams AL, Dohring EJ, et al. Lumbar disc nomenclature: version 2.0: recommendations of the combined task forces of the North American Spine Society, the American Society of Spine Radiology and the American Society of Neuroradiology. Spine J. 2014;14(11):2525-2545.

stem: Which of the following findings can suggest screw loosening in patients with fusion hardware?

options.text: >1 mm lucency around the screw margin in radiographs
>2 mm lucency around the screw margin
Presence of any lucency around the screw margin in radiographs

explanation: Answer C. >2 mm lucency around the screw margin (more so in radiographs) is generally considered a sign of screw loosening, which may occur because of abnormal motion or infection that may lead to hardware failure. Any lucency on CT irrespective of its size should still be considered with caution.

stem: What is an indication of possible motion across the fused levels?
explanation:
Answer B. Centrally interrupted trabeculation. These can be seen with abnormal motion across the fused segment and may result in the loosening of hardware. Imaging Findings: There is a lucency surrounding the screw tract that is >2 mm, suggestive of screw loosening. Implant Loosening Discussion: Loosening of hardware is an indication of potential hardware failure and abnormal motion across the fused segments. It may be seen as an enlarging lucency around the screw tract or a lucency of >2 mm surrounding it. This can lead to hardware fracture, subsidence (collapse of the cage devices through the endplate), or migration. Complications of spinal fixation can be classified into early and late complications. Early complications include the following: • Hematoma • Seroma • CSF leak • Infection • Hardware malposition Late complications include the following: • Hardware failure or fracture • Hardware loosening • Hardware migration • Damage to adjacent structures • Pseudoarthrosis and nonunion • Adjacent segment accelerated degeneration Postoperative CT and MR remain a cornerstone for the assessment of hardware integrity and degree of anatomic fusion. Typically, osseous fusion can be identified on CT in the form of osseous trabeculae bridging across the fused segments. Disruption of this trabeculation is an indication of abnormal motion and may result in hardware failure. Formation of pseudoarthrosis and infection are other etiologies for hardware failure, which can be evaluated on both CT and MRI. MRI is particularly sensitive to the detection of infection, arachnoiditis, or postoperative fibrosis. In addition, recurrent disk herniations, abnormal stress on the facet articulations, and worsening listhesis can be detected.

References: Malhotra A, Kalra VB, Wu X, et al. Imaging of lumbar spinal surgery complications. Insights Imaging. 2015;6(6):579-590.

stem: A 65-year-old female had recent kyphoplasty. What abnormality is noted on this CT?
explanation:
Answer A. Cement embolism. There are curvilinear branching hyperdensities in the pulmonary vasculature. Note there is cement material in the perivertebral venous plexus. Although the other entities can be associated with pulmonary calcifications, the morphology and clinical scenario are most compatible with cement embolism. Imaging Findings: There are branching hyperdensities (arrows) that are seen in the setting of recent kyphoplasty with presence of cement in the perivertebral venous plexus (dotted arrow) most compatible with cement embolism. Cement Embolism Discussion: Percutaneous vertebral kyphoplasty and vertebroplasty are techniques used for vertebral augmentation and treatment of painful compression fractures. Vertebroplasty was first employed using a transpedicular approach to introduce cement (polymethylmethacrylate [PMMA]) into the affected vertebra. Subsequently, this technique was modified using a balloon kyphoplasty approach that employs inflation of the balloon to expand the compression fracture and create a void for cement injection. These interventions have been found to aid correction of loss of vertebral body height, angular kyphosis, pain, and overall functional state. Although rare, serious complications such as extrusion of cement material in the spinal canal or neural foramina resulting in cord compression or nerve root compression or venous infiltration resulting in pulmonary embolism as seen in this case may occur.

References: Yaltirik K, Ashour AM, Reis CR, et al. Vertebral augmentation by kyphoplasty and vertebroplasty: 8 years experience outcomes and complications. J Craniovertebr Junction Spine. 2016;7(3):153-160.

stem: A patient presented with worsening right lower extremity radiculopathy. Based on these MR images, what is the likely etiology?

options.text: Right facet septic arthritis with abscess
Right S1 nerve root perineural cyst

explanation:
Answer B. Right subarticular zone synovial cyst. Imaging findings: T2 hyperintense lesion medial to the right L5-S1 facet joint, causing effacement of lateral recess and encroachment of the traversing right S1 nerve root. There is no edema or signs of inflammation to suggest infection. Perineural cyst typically has no mass effect on the nerve and is centered around the nerve. This lesion is cystic unlike a sequestered disc. Synovial Cyst Discussion: Spinal synovial cysts are associated with facet degeneration, most seen at L4-L5 (the second most common site is L5-S1). They can be medial, lateral, or posterior to the facet joint. They can cause radiculopathy or claudication. Typically, they are T2 hyperintense, are T1 hypointense, and can show peripheral enhancement. Communication with the joint space can be seen. The major differential diagnosis is disk extrusion.

References: Apostolaki E, Davies AM, Evans N, Cassar-Pullicino VN. MR imaging of lumbar facet joint synovial cysts. Eur Radiol. 2000;10(4):615-623.

stem: A patient presented with myelopathy symptoms after minor trauma. T2 and gradient images are provided. What is the hypointense structure that has a significant contribution to spinal canal stenosis along the posterior vertebral body margin?
explanation:
Answer B. Ossified posterior longitudinal ligament. Imaging Findings: Hypointense structure along the posterior aspect of vertebrae, spanning bodies as well as disk spaces. In axial gradient images, some blooming and "bowtie" configuration is evident. Findings are most consistent with ossification of the posterior longitudinal ligament. This is confirmed on the provided CT. Changes of diffuse skeletal idiopathic hyperostosis are also evident. Ossification of Posterior Longitudinal Ligament Discussion: Calcification/ossification of the posterior longitudinal ligament shows greater prevalence in males, the elderly, and the Japanese Asian population. The exact pathogenesis is unclear. It is mostly seen in the cervical spine and can contribute to canal stenosis, predisposing to spinal cord injury even from minor trauma. It can be contiguous over several vertebral segments, or localized/segmental. On MRI, a hypointense structure is seen in the anterior aspect of the spinal canal spanning the vertebral body as well as disk space. This differs from the usual location of osteophytes, which are typically found at the level of the disk space. In cases with well-formed marrow within the ligament, it can show signal characteristics of bone (hyperintense on T1 images). Axial gradient images can show blooming. Inverted T or bowtie configuration can be evident on axial images. CT would be a confirmatory test and is generally needed for surgical planning. OPLL can be associated with diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (as in this case), ossification of ligamentum flavum, and ankylosing spondylitis.

References: Choi BW, Song KJ, Chang H. Ossification of the posterior longitudinal ligament: a review of literature. Asian Spine J. 2011;5(4):267-276.
